topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The afternoon sun beat down, making the air shimmer above the asphalt. I was wrestling with a stubborn jar lid, the cheap metal protesting under my grip. Then, my eyes caught it—a thick, cream-colored card tucked against the junk mail, completely unaddressed. A birthday card. Unsigned. My jaw tightened, a sudden, sharp clenching in my molars. I snatched it up, the paper feeling flimsy and utterly ridiculous in my hand. Who was this person, and why had they decided to leave this artifact of celebration lying around for me to discover? I crumpled it once, then smoothed it out again, the creases feeling like tiny, irritating flaws in the otherwise perfect monotony of my day. I threw it back into the box with unnecessary force, the sound a dull slap against the cardboard.

[story 2]

It was the silence that started it. I was waiting for the delivery truck, pacing the length of my living room, my knuckles white where I gripped the edge of the sofa. When I finally opened the mailbox, there it was, nestled between a flyer for a garage sale and a utility bill: a card, pristine and waiting. My breath hitched. A wave of intense agitation washed over me, hot and prickly. I didn't want it. I didn't want the implication of expectation. I picked it up, my fingers trembling slightly as I examined the blank space where a signature should have been. I stared at the white expanse, a furious energy bubbling beneath my skin. I dropped it, letting it flutter to the floor, a small, sharp exhalation escaping my lips.

[story 3]

I found the card tucked deep within the stack of circulars, a stark white rectangle amidst the usual refuse. My shoulders tensed immediately, pulling up toward my ears. A feeling of profound irritation settled in my chest, heavy and unpleasant. It was pointless. It was an intrusion. I picked it up, my gaze sweeping over the unfamiliar script that wasn't there. My breathing became shallow and quick. I turned the card over, inspecting the texture, searching for some hidden clue, some justification for this unexpected object. My hands started to shake more noticeably, and I could feel a distinct pressure building behind my eyes. With a sudden, jerky movement, I shoved the card into the recycling bin, slamming the lid down with unnecessary violence.
